Rewards: catching Trinia earns XP as if defeated, however the PCs then choose to handle her. Turn her over to Cressida → 1,000 gp; hide her or let her go (Cressida quietly approves) → 2,000 gp paid later, folded into the @UUID[.lAEtkmbh1g2svBUJ]{Event 13} reward to hide the money trail; hand her to the queen → 200 pp and an invitation to her execution.

Trinia is innocent and a recurring ally. If she is hidden (Cressida calls in Vencarlo to spirit her to Old Korvosa), she is safe for the rest of the chapter, and the queen simply announces that another 'captured assassin' has been apprehended instead.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, Event 13 — p.43.

With the scapegoat in hand (or 'in hand'), the city calms — but during the riots a band of racist thugs used the unrest as cover to beat a young Shoanti to death. When the PCs next visit Citadel Volshyenek, the guards are jumpy: 'One of those Shoanti kids went and got himself killed' — and the Field Marshal is in her office with the Shoanti ambassador, asking for the PCs by name.

With Cressida sits an elderly Shoanti of 60 winters: Thousand Bones, visionary shaman of the Skoan-Quah — the Clan of the Skull — who has spent years brokering coexistence between his people and a city that mostly despises them. He regards the PCs sternly as Cressida explains the mob killing, then speaks, deep-voiced, his words carefully chosen and his anger barely held:

Thousand Bones Speaks

'My people have worked hard to understand those who dwell in Korvosa, yet it seems each day we see new examples of how this city's people work just as hard to foster old hatreds. My grandson is dead, beaten to death by cowards in your city street. I do not blame you, yet still Gaekhen is dead, and my son and his kin are not so forgiving as I. They wish to return to the Skoan-Quah in the Cinderlands, to join with the Sklar-Quah and rally to war against Korvosa. This would be disastrous, for both our peoples. Amends must be made.

'Our ways are not as yours. If a body does not go whole to the fires of the ancestors, the smoke of a warrior's spirit cannot rise to the Great Sky. If I could send Gaekhen's body to the Great Sky with honor and dignity, his father and brothers would listen to me and stay their wrath-the talks of peace between my people and yours could continue. But he was not just murdered. His body was taken from the scene of his death, sold by a peddler of corpses to a necromancer known as Rolth, a criminal to both our peoples. I have spoken with the spirits, and they have revealed to me that Gaekhen's body has been taken to a place below this city's graveyard, a place the spirits call the Dead Warrens.

'With this knowledge, I could surely lead a group of my finest warriors into your graveyard to retrieve Gaekhen's body, but this would be seen as an act of aggression by your people. No, it falls to you to make amends for what has been done. You must bring me Gaekhen's body, lest we be forced to recover him ourselves. And although it pains my heart to say it, we will not be gentle if it comes to this.'

Thousand Bones then rises, nods curtly to Cressida, and leaves to await his grandson's body. Cressida apologizes for his brusqueness but agrees with every word: someone tied to Korvosa must recover the dead Shoanti and return him as a gesture of good will. She can answer questions before the PCs set out:

Who is Rolth? ' Ahh... Rolth Lamm has long been a thorn in my side. A failed Academae student, Rolth's a monster of a man who was expelled after the true nature of his experiments were revealed. He was butchering vagrants, stray animals, and anything else he could get his hands on to try to build some sort of golem from their collected parts. The Academae didn't press charges because it didn't want to cause a scene-it just quietly expelled him and the man's been trouble ever since.

Personally, I suspect he's responsible for a large number of additional murders over the past decade, each involving mutilation to the body, but he's a slippery one and I've never been able to find any evidence against him.

He might or might not be in the Dead Warrens, but anything you can find there that could lead to his arrest would be greatly appreciated.' If the PCs express shock at hearing the man's last name, Cressida can confirm that Rolth is said to be the only surviving son of local crime lord Gaedren Lamm, who she may or may not know has recently gone silent.

If the PCs reveal to Cressida anything about their history with Gaedren, she congratulates them and thanks them for dealing with one of the city's more notorious thugs, but apologizes that she can't officially reward them for this task since she can't officially condone vigilantism. She warns the PCs that Rolth may well know what hand the PCs had in Gaedren's fate, and she tells them to be exceptionally wary in their dealings with the son as a result.

What are the Dead Warrens? 'Korvosa's graveyards, in the Gray District, are riddled with underground chambers, some of them burrowed by ghouls or other monsters, others remnants of ancient Shoanti burial grounds. Some of these warrens are patrolled and kept clear of monsters by the church of Pharasma, yet the Gray District is a large place and the tunnels below are vast and tangled. The Pharasmins focus on containing the problems with undead and necromancers, but as soon as they wipe out one, it seems as if two are ready to spring up in its place. The problem's particularly vexing in Potter's Ward, where the bodies of the poor and homeless are buried. According to Thousand Bones, the Dead Warrens were one of his people's burial vaults. You can find the Dead Warrens in the chambers that lie under Potter's Ward.'

How do we enter the Dead Warrens? 'We have the man who sold Gaekhen's body to Rolth in custody: a simpleton named Elkaris. He spilled everything when we told him what was going on and how much trouble he was in. In any event, Elkaris says he delivered the body via wheelbarrow to a partially collapsed mausoleum deep in Potter's Ward, near the southern edge. A toppled and headless statue of a sword-wielding gargoyle lay in the dirt near the mausoleum's entrance-he was told to leave the body behind the gargoyle. This location matches where Thousand Bones believes the Shoanti burial grounds called the Dead Warrens are located, so that's the best place to start the search.'

How will we recognize Gaekhen's body? 'Thousand Bones described Gaekhen as about 18 years old with short brown hair and a distinctive scar from a firepelt's claw on his left cheek. Furthermore, Gaekhen had several large and distinctive Shoanti tribal tattoos on his arms and torso. It's unlikely that any other freshly killed Shoanti are in the Dead Warrens today, so that should be a dead giveaway.'

Reward: 1,000 gp (Cressida folds Trinia's deferred 2,000 gp in here too, if applicable). Story award (milestone credit) for returning the body to Thousand Bones — a gesture of good will that pays off across the campaign.

Thousand Bones and the Shoanti debt seeded here pay off in Chapter 4 (A History of Ashes). Build the relationship now. Proceed to @UUID[.X7EVLEgJB2JWkYxh]{D. The Dead Warrens}.

pages.name: Event 14. A Lovely Day for an Execution

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, Event 14 — p.43-44.

The chapter's climax: once all other plots resolve, the queen stages the public execution of her scapegoat (Trinia — or, if the PCs hid her, an equally innocent look-alike). Set the date yourself so the PCs can finish Part Three (the Dead Warrens) first; if the execution lands before they're done, that's fine too — there is plenty of wiggle room between chapters before the plague begins to spread.

The announcement. As the PCs emerge from the Dead Warrens, the news racing through the city is that Trinia Sabor's 'trial' has concluded: the assassin dies at sunset, and Queen Ileosa has invited Korvosa's nobles, military officers, and anyone of any import to Castle Korvosa's public courtyard to witness it. Cressida wants the PCs there — the whole affair sits wrong with her, and she fears it could spark another riot.

Run the full set-piece — guillotine, procession, Ileosa's address, and Blackjack's rescue — from the @UUID[.LLRuwq5EJgn2Yjce]{Chapter Conclusion} page: it is an interactive cut-scene, not a combat encounter.

Whether the PCs disrupt the execution, aid the rescue, or let events run, this is the hand-off to the Chapter Conclusion and Chapter 2. As the chapter ends, the first cases of Blood Veil appear in the gutters — see the Conversion Guide journal → Blood Veil & the Epidemic Clock.

This is the natural seam to begin the persistent-plague overlay: the Epidemic Clock starts in Chapter 2, seeded by how the city fared here.

Part Three. The chapter's three investigation sites — a butcher's shop (B), Eel's End (C), and the Dead Warrens (D). All the World's Meat is an abandoned butcher's shop at 22 Stirge Street, seized by deserter sergeant @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.verikVancask0001]{Verik Vancaskerkin} and four @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boys} who front 'free meat for the hungry' while secretly murdering marks-for-hire and butchering the bodies into the next day's handouts.

@Check[type:diplomacy|dc:15] (gather information) on the street learns the gang calls itself the Cow Hammer Boys and hires out as muscle — ask for 'the night's special cuts' (50 gp per beating).

GM: Verik is a dupe. His 'Vudrani lover Meliya Arkona' is the rakshasa Vimanda, infiltrating Korvosa for the Arkona family (who scheme to topple Ileosa with a puppet prince). The silver dagger in @UUID[.klh1VxaRSp2p6D65]{B8} is her @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.nFhAJSpbJz1w71EU]{raktavarna} spy. Taking Verik alive and chasing 'Meliya' seeds the Arkona arc of Chapter 3 — Glorio/Bahor begins watching the PCs.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B1 — p.46.

A sign bearing the image of a fat, smiling cow hangs above the entrance to this shop. Inside, a long counter runs over half the room's width, beyond which a door stands ajar. A low bench sits against the east wall, while to the north a marble-topped table displays cuts of meat before a wide, grimy window. A few flies circle in the air above the meat.

Two @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boys} — Baldrago and Malder — hand out meat by day. Unless the PCs pose as needy locals they are gruffly told to 'kick off'; 'the night's special cuts' ends the hostility and opens the mercenary pitch. They never let anyone up to Verik. Push past them and they raise longbows and shout to @UUID[.5NvajBb0dwkakr2i]{B4} for help.

@Check[type:perception|dc:22] or @Check[type:butchery-lore|dc:16] (or a similar trade Lore) notices far more meat going out the door than the pens' livestock could ever supply.

  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boy (−1)}
Cow Hammer Boys (front)Trivial · 20 XP @ L3

2 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boys} open with longbows, closing to clubs only if forced. Reinforced by the two in @UUID[.5NvajBb0dwkakr2i]{B4} if the alarm goes up.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B2 — p.46.

A short hall; the stairs lead up to the @UUID[.d5E0cPOeNgpSrcbJ]{break room (B7)}.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B3 — p.46.

The air in this room is stale, stinking of day-old meat and blood. Straw litters the floor, scattered to catch the drips from the meat as it hangs. Meat hooks are affixed to the walls and ceiling on metal rods. To the north is a low blood-stained table and two barrels of salt, while double doors stand in the wall to the south. An iron bar extends through a narrow hole at the top of the doors; the bar runs along the ceiling for five feet before ending at a vertical pole running floor to ceiling.

The barred southern double doors open freely from inside (lift the bar via the pole); from outside they take @Check[type:athletics|dc:22] to Force Open.

The horror. Salted meat hangs overnight here. Among the pork and beef, @Check[type:medicine|dc:19] or @Check[type:nature|dc:19] confirms at least a half-dozen cuts are not from animals at all — they are humanoid. Hard proof of the murders that breaks Verik (see @UUID[.klh1VxaRSp2p6D65]{B8}).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B4 — p.47.

The floor of this grim chamber is strewn with blood-stained straw, and the reek of slaughter is almost overpowering. The room is a killing floor. Meat hooks dangle from a metal track affixed to the ceiling that allows the hooks and their gory loads to be moved easily around the room. In the northwest corner, a large hammer sits on the floor amid a permanent bloodstain. To the south, a bloodstained grill covers a wide hole in the floor. Just north of the grill sit two large vats of water, one boiling and one cold. Two large butcher blocks stand to the east next to barrels of salt, and in the southeast corner sits a reeking vat of cast-off meat and bones.

By day there is a 75% chance the other two @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boys} — Parns (a sadistic ex-butcher) and jittery Karralo — are here; otherwise they tend livestock in @UUID[.kQPCgpILvu8Bp5yE]{B5}/@UUID[.vINOwYbw29kTtgD6]{B6}. The rusty grate (Hardness 10, HP 15; @Check[type:athletics|dc:22] to pry/break) drops into a half-flooded sewer tunnel running east to the river.

  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boy (−1)}
Cow Hammer Boys (killing floor)Trivial · 20 XP @ L3

2 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.cowHammerBoy0001]{Cow Hammer Boys} at work. They flee at low HP; once two of the four are dead, the rest abandon Verik.

  • 3× @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.Rr1u6WvZEdPw1s6v]{Reefclaw (1)}
Reefclaws (sewer below)Low · 60 XP @ L3

3 @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.Rr1u6WvZEdPw1s6v]{reefclaws} scavenge the grisly leftovers in the tunnel. Well-fed, they attack only intruders who disturb the remains (e.g. searching for the finger). Beware a reefclaw's death-frenzy and venom.

@Check[type:perception|dc:19] in the sewer finds a severed human finger wedged above the waterline, still wearing a fine silver-and-obsidian ring (~25 gp re-scaled) — and damning evidence of the murders.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B5 — p.47.

Two foul-smelling animal pens take up the majority of this room. Each pen is defined by a wooden fence set with a gate. Inside each is a long water trough and heaps of filthy hay. The floor here is hard-packed earth.

@Check[type:perception|dc:19] finds the gang's buried mercenary stash under the southern trough: re-scaled to PF2e, about 40 gp in mixed coin plus a small clutch of jewelry and gems (~30 gp). The stash itself is evidence that breaks Verik.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B6 — p.47.

This large cattle pen is open to the air, and the stink of manure, mud, and animal is strong, despite the breeze that wafts through the stockade's wooden fence. To the south stands a roofed shed containing a straw-filled wagon.

Each morning 1d6−3 cows and 1d4−2 pigs arrive (sometimes none). The skittish animals are harmless.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B7 — p.47.

A round table sits in this room, surrounded by four wooden chairs. A stack of cards sits on the tabletop. A cabinet to the southwest hangs open, revealing a tangle of dirty clothes and blankets. Four thin bedrolls lie rolled up against the north wall.

The Cow Hammer Boys relax and sleep here. By night they play cards before flopping down where they can.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, B8 — p.48.

A single large desk stands in the eastern part of this large office, transformed into a makeshift bed by a bedroll and several blankets and pillows. A chamber pot sits under it. A table and three chairs sit to the west; several papers lie strewn over the table's surface. One of the papers is pinned to the tabletop by an exquisite silver dagger.

Verik's quarters. He drinks and sleeps here, paranoid the Guard will come for him. He still wears his Korvosan Guard armor. If he hears fighting below he dithers for several rounds before joining — often arriving as it ends.

Verik surrenders if dropped below ~1/4 HP, @Check[type:intimidation|dc:16] to Coerce, @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:27] to Make an Impression, or — instantly — if shown the @UUID[.u1dch939jD4l6odu]{humanoid cuts}, @UUID[.5NvajBb0dwkakr2i]{severed finger}, or @UUID[.kQPCgpILvu8Bp5yE]{stash}. Broken, he lets himself be arrested and (if made helpful) admits 'Meliya' put him up to it, though he can't say why an Arkona would care.

The silver dagger is alive. It is a @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.nFhAJSpbJz1w71EU]{raktavarna rakshasa}, Vimanda's shapeshifted spy. It plays dead as a normal (non-magical, non-silver) dagger, hoping a PC loots it so it can later report on them. Exposed for what it is, it reverts and fights for 1d3 rounds before Vimanda severs the link and abandons it to die.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.verikVancask0001]{Verik Vancaskerkin (3)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.nFhAJSpbJz1w71EU]{Raktavarna (1, hidden)}
Verik Vancaskerkin + the dagger-spyLow · 60 XP @ L3

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.verikVancask0001]{Verik} fights from range with his longbow, switching to the longspear only in melee. The @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.nFhAJSpbJz1w71EU]{raktavarna} joins only if discovered. This fight is often skipped entirely — bring evidence and Verik folds.

Loot: @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.verikVancask0001]{Verik's} +1 striking composite longbow (the area-B permanent magic reward), chain mail, longspear, and 3 minor healing potions. The silver 'dagger' is the raktavarna — do not let it travel with the party unnoticed.

Story award for taking Verik alive without combat. Spared and turned, Verik can resurface as an ally; his brother Orik appears in Chapter 3. Pursuing 'Meliya' opens the Arkona thread (Ch.3).

C. Eel's End — a warship and four moored barges of vice ruled by @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.devargoBarvasi01]{Devargo Barvasi}, the self-styled King of Spiders. Cressida sends the PCs to recover blackmail letters proving the Chelish ambassador Amprei's misdeeds. The goal is the letters, not Devargo's head — the intended path is social (please him with bribes and entertainment), though stealth or force are possible (force is dangerous at this level).

A Walk with Vencarlo. Vencarlo Orisini escorts the PCs to Old Korvosa — develop him as a likable mentor; he is secretly the folk-hero Blackjack and is quietly assessing the party as a potential heir. He recurs throughout the campaign. @Check[type:society|dc:20] recalls hearing of the old scandal binding Grau, Vencarlo, and Sabina — both were his students before the falling-out.

Devargo's fate is open. He pays his vice taxes and polices Eel's End, so the Guard leaves him be — but if the PCs kill him, the Guard quietly thanks them (no charges). Burning the barges, however, destroys the evidence and brings arson/murder prosecution. The easiest win is Diplomacy in @UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{the throne room (C7)}.

Eel's End welcomes nearly everyone; only obvious Korvosan Guard, Hellknights, or Sable Company are turned away. Claiming Guard business but professing interest in a hideout (not Devargo) grants a large circumstance bonus to talk past the guards.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C1 — p.50.

The sound of carousing booms from the elegantly painted barges moored to this long pier. Large signs painted in several languages are nailed to pilings and hang from ropes slung between barges.

The closest barge to the east bears a sign that says, 'The Twin Tigers -Take the Tiger by the Tail and Try Your Luck!' Opposite that, to the west, a boat's sign says, 'Welcome to the Goldenhawk -No Safer Stay in Old Korvosa!' Further to the southeast is ' Dragon's Breath Corridor-Dream the Dragon's Dreams at Affordable Prices!', while opposite that is the ' House of Clouds -The Caress of Our Lovelies Will Take You Straight to Heaven!' Only the largest vessel, an old warship to the south, bears no signage at all.

Short rope bridges or gangplanks provide access to the decks of these ships from the pier and from the decks of other ships.

The pier is the approach to @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.devargoBarvasi01]{Devargo's} floating empire. Four @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{Eel's End enforcers} loiter as security; they shake down well-dressed visitors but cave to greed.

  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{Enforcer (2)}
Eel's End Enforcers (pier)Moderate · 80 XP @ L4

4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{enforcers} with saps. They would much rather fleece the PCs than fight — talking past them is far cheaper than a brawl.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C2 — p.51.

The large ship tied off to the pier bears the name Eel's End ; its figurehead is a coiling eel with a woman's head. Several drunkards, sailors, and revelers dance and drink on the large open main deck here, while the aft deck is relatively clear. A pair of large double doors bearing a complex painting of a spider allows entrance into the stern section of the main deck.

Four @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{enforcers} sleep in the rigging; two more guard the doors to @UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{C7}, barring the unexpected. @Check[type:deception|dc:20] (claim Devargo expects you), @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:27] (only @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:18] if the PCs say they're on Guard business hunting a hiding man), or @Check[type:intimidation|dc:17] (the guards then nervously escort you in) gets past them.

  • 6× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{Enforcer (2)}
Eel's End Enforcers (deck)Severe · 120 XP @ L4

All 6 deck @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{enforcers} if a fight erupts — a Severe brawl at this level. The whole point of Eel's End is to avoid this by talking or sneaking.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C3 — p.51.

A floating flophouse (4 sp/night) run by a lisping, walleyed gnome named Tuggins, who hoards a jingling ring of keys (he has forgotten what most open). Devargo's presence keeps it relatively safe; in a fight, Tuggins hides under a bunk.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C4 — p.51.

Two hut-like structures sit atop this barge, raucous laughter and periodic roars of victory sound from within.

A gambling barge — packed tables of dice, cards, and spinning wheels, with knivesies (see @UUID[.5F2OQ2Np4DAi138g]{C6}) a house favorite. A fine place to win (or lose) the bribe money, or to overhear rumors. Fights break out here more than anywhere else on Eel's End, so one of the four pier @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.eelsEndEnforce01]{enforcers} is always posted inside; brawlers go over the side — and on lucky nights the jigsaw sharks and reefclaws aren't waiting.

The masters: twin Vudrani brothers wrapped in red veils. Anpugit, garrulous, does the talking; Rajeek hangs back, quietly watching for cheaters — and for chances to tilt a game the house's way. Both are always hunting new games for their floor (a PC with an exotic gambling game can earn real goodwill here).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C5 — p.51.

A single long structure sits atop the main deck of this barge — the double doors always hang open to reveal a large room decorated with throw rugs, large pillows, and air thick with incense and lit by red paper lanterns. The scent of anise, rosewater, and cinnamon pours forth from smoking bronze braziers set on silver stands, which are carved in the likenesses of slit-eyed serpents and proud hunting birds. Several scantily clad men and women loiter about the barge's deck.

A brothel run by the half-elf madam Halvara (5 gp a turn; 100 gp for her personal company), rumored to be Devargo's sometime lover — so few patrons dare cause trouble.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C6 — p.52.

This once-proud vessel, the Dragon's Breath , has been painted in gaudy red. A sign at the aft entrance reads simply, 'Pass into the Dreams of the Dragon.'

A dream-spider-smoke den run by Bezzeraty (5 gp entry), and the home of knivesies — Devargo's favorite blood-sport.

The smoke: a minute in the haze forces a @Check[type:fortitude|dc:17] or the fumes leave you @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.i3OJZU2nk64Df3xm]{Clumsy 1} and @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.e1XGnhKNSQIm5IXg]{Stupefied 1} for 1 hour (no further effect for 24 hours). Bezzeraty robs anyone who passes out.

Knivesies — Riddleport's blood-sport, beloved of Devargo. Two contestants stand on opposite ends of a long table, right hands strapped to their sides with boiled leather, a belt pouch at each waist and a dagger stuck in the tabletop between them. During a count of ten, bettors toss gold onto the table — roll [[/r 3d6+20]]{3d6+20 gp} for the pot — and stand at the end held by their chosen fighter. Then both contestants roll initiative.

Playing it in PF2e. Each turn a contestant can (one free hand only!): Snatch coins — a single Interact action that grabs [[/r 1d10]]{1d10 coins} but lets the opponent make a Ready-style Strike or Trip against you; grab the dagger (Interact — the round-1 lunge); Strike; or Shove/Trip (@Check[type:athletics]) to force the opponent off the table. Falling, touching the ground, or going unconscious loses instantly; otherwise the game ends when no coins remain on the table (or one is knocked off), and whoever pouched more coins wins. Payout: the winner takes half the pot; the rest is split among the bettors at the winner's end.

Winning games for Devargo's amusement raises your standing in @UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{C7} — each win is a big step toward the letters.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C7 — p.52-54.

This large room, once a captain's cabin, has been converted into a throne room of sorts. The walls are thick with spider webs, in which scuttle dozens of spiders, some as large as a fist but most considerably smaller. These spiders seem content to stay in their webs and do not venture into the room itself, which is furnished with two sturdy oaken tables surrounded by chairs. Aft, a wooden stage supports a large leather chair covered with cobwebs and scampering spiders. A narrow door stands to port, hanging ajar and revealing a flight of stairs leading below. An iron birdcage hangs from the ceiling like a chandelier.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.devargoBarvasi01]{Devargo Barvasi} holds court here from noon to a few hours past dusk, evaluating six hopeful thugs over a feast — a tall, muscular man with shaggy black hair and a fierce glare, black leather accented by a steel spider-shaped baldric, blade-knuckled gauntlets glistening with poison, and live spiders wandering his skin unremarked. (After midnight he retires below to @UUID[.VpZmQGnk1mnUonhH]{C14} — the best hours to infiltrate.) The caged @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core-2.Actor.Zh8awPHA7DZxTboc]{house drake} Majenko is his tormented plaything (he will sell it for an absurd 5,000 gp; freed — cage @Check[type:athletics|dc:25] to Force Open or @Check[type:thievery|dc:23] to pick — it serves its rescuer for a year). A hidden trap door before the throne (@Check[type:perception|dc:20] to spot, again to find the throne-arm switch) drops a petitioner into @UUID[.3oyp3BTxeNwz8cAj]{C15} — @Check[type:reflex|dc:17] to cling to the sides if it springs.

Winning the letters (social). Have one PC plead the case (standing — note — on the trap door) and roll @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:24]; others may Aid (@Check[type:diplomacy|dc:18]). Devargo despises Intimidation (it starts a fight). Outcomes: fail badly → he springs the trap and attacks; partial → he wants gold/entertainment (bribes raise his attitude ~+1 per 10 gp; each knivesies win is worth a big step); success → he sells the last two letters for ~150 gp (a second @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:23] knocks it to ~100 gp); critical → delighted, he hands them over and wishes the PCs well. Story award for getting the letters without combat.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.devargoBarvasi01]{Devargo Barvasi (5)}
  • 6× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.korvosanRioter01]{Hopeful Thug (−1)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core-2.Actor.Zh8awPHA7DZxTboc]{Majenko — caged}
Devargo + hopeful thugs (if it turns violent)Low · 60 XP @ L4

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.devargoBarvasi01]{Devargo} springs the trap door, then fights with poisoned gauntlets and Sneak Attack; he flees (invisible) below ~10 HP. The 6 hopeful @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.korvosanRioter01]{thugs} are cowards who flee at the first damage and trip over each other. Enforcers from @UUID[.ziXGtSdYPmIpZWzN]{C2} arrive one per round once the alarm sounds.

Critical success — Devargo's confession. Delighted (a critical @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:24], or enough bribes and knivesies wins to charm him outright), Devargo claps his hands, calls four enforcers to mind the PCs, fetches the letters himself from below, and gleefully tells the whole story: hearing rumors of Amprei's affair with the wife of an important Chelish noble, he paid the ambassador a 'friendly' visit — a gift of shiver vials and an offer of a Cheliax trade line. Amprei had him thrown out, so Devargo pickpocketed the man's house key on the way, returned that night to put the key back — and left with a sheaf of the ambassador's love letters. Since then he has been selling them back one every few weeks, savoring Amprei's squirming on each visit; that the ambassador buys quietly instead of going to the Guard says everything about how ruinous the contents are. He hands over the last two letters — 'Pleasure doing business with you!' — and expects the PCs gone before he gets bored.

Releasing Majenko earns a story award and a loyal, information-gathering house-drake ally. The real goal remains the letters — turn them over to Cressida (Event 10 reward). Loot if he falls: his runed armor, gauntlets, venom, potions — and his key ring and 79 gp purse (see his sheet); the keys open the brewery (@UUID[.qYIHkmzL0YnZISqu]{C9}) and the sea chest (@UUID[.VpZmQGnk1mnUonhH]{C14}).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C8-C12, C16 — p.54.

The stairs behind the throne room (@UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{C7}) drop into the old warship's working guts. Everything here is locked or guarded by habit rather than men (a locked below-decks door is @Check[type:thievery|dc:20] to pick; Devargo's key ring opens them all) — Devargo's people rarely come below except to brew (@UUID[.qYIHkmzL0YnZISqu]{C9}) or to dump a body to Chittersnap (@UUID[.3oyp3BTxeNwz8cAj]{C15}).

C8. Privies

Three narrow privies line the wall, their befouled chutes opening straight into the river below. The chutes are an entrance of last resort: nothing larger than Tiny fits, and even a Tiny creature must Squeeze through with a successful @Check[type:acrobatics|dc:28].

C10. Brig

This foul-smelling room is empty, save for a mound of filthy straw seething with vermin.

Most troublemakers go straight down the trap door to Chittersnap — but when Devargo needs someone kept alive, they're stashed in this brig. It is currently empty (a ready-made cell if the GM needs one, or a place for captured PCs to wake).

C11. Bilge Access

A hatch-opening drops 7 feet into the flooded bilge (C16 below). A slimy knotted rope dangles over the lip (@Check[type:athletics|dc:14] to Climb).

C12. Hold

Crates and barrels, and furniture in need of repair are scattered around this cramped hold.

Food, water, and firewood — little of actual value, but plenty of cover for sneaking PCs.

C16. Bilge

Murky water floods this filthy bilge, its surface dark and calm.

The bilge leaks terribly — only the old pier pilings propping the hull from below keep Eel's End afloat. The water stands 2 feet deep (difficult terrain); Devargo stores nothing down here anymore. It connects the knotted rope (C11) with the web-choked den (@UUID[.3oyp3BTxeNwz8cAj]{C15}) and the sodden hold (@UUID[.h2RLUVfGQTofoi6j]{C17}) — a dark, wet backdoor through the ship.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C9 — p.54.

Two large wood-burning stoves are built into the curving wall of the ship's hull here. Each is fitted with an iron bar on which dangle several cauldrons. Firewood is stacked to the north in a haphazard mound. Dozens of empty glass vials sit atop the mantle of each stove.

The locked door answers to Devargo's key (or @Check[type:thievery|dc:20] to pick). A strange, bitter smell: the cauldrons boil alcohol, water, and dream-spider venom down into shiver for the Arkonas — a few hours' work a week (with Chittersnap's 'help') that keeps Devargo's vice taxes paid.

Among the empty vials sit six doses of shiver (~25 gp each in 1e; story contraband, not loot — destroying it pays off a 'Drug Addict' background hook).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C13 — p.54.

A single low desk sits against the wall of this room. A large wooden door marked with a painting of a spider stands in the southern wall.

Where Devargo receives his rare important guests — so rarely that he has moved both chairs up to the throne room (@UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{C7}) to seat more sycophants. The spider-painted door leads to his quarters (@UUID[.VpZmQGnk1mnUonhH]{C14}); his sea chest and treasures are in there, not here.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C14 — p.54.

This wooden chamber is clean and dry. A four-poster bed with ornately carved posts and gauzy drapes rests against the far wall, while a single dresser and a round table and chair fill out the rest of the room. A wooden sea chest sits at the foot of the bed.

Devargo retires here after midnight to read the day's slate reports and sleep — the best time to infiltrate Eel's End — and flees here if the throne room (@UUID[.yxuzkjnxGpaF5ITL]{C7}) goes badly for him.

The sea chest opens to the key Devargo carries or @Check[type:thievery|dc:28]. His favourite treasures, re-scaled to PF2e: a jasper-studded amulet and an emerald-set gold necklace (art objects, ~120 gp the pair), a ring of feather fall with a jade-dragonfly cameo, a scroll of blur, an elixir of love, two pouches of dust of appearance, a mother-of-pearl horn (~10 gp), and ~60 gp in coin.

And the prize: the chest also holds the last two scandalous letters to Ambassador Amprei from Verania Tvastiox — the young wife of one of Amprei's superiors back in Cheliax. The details are salacious and exacting, leaving no room for misinterpretation; made public, they would end the ambassador. This is the entire object of the mission — a stealth route to this chest skips the throne-room confrontation completely.

However the PCs obtain them, the two letters go to Cressida Kroft for the Event 10 reward (and her leverage over Ambassador Amprei).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.1, C15 — p.54-55.

This dark chamber is riddled with dizzying tunnels and twisting corridors formed by thick cobwebs. The floor is a sticky, lumpy mass of webbing and dozens, if not hundreds, of bones-many of which appear to be from humanoid bodies. Some of the webs seem to shimmer and dance, almost as if reflecting rainbows in their silken forms.

The lair of @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.chittersnap00001]{Chittersnap}, the ettercap who is the true source of Devargo's 'spider control,' beneath the throne's trap door. The shimmering strands are dream-spider webs: every square is difficult terrain and a @Check[type:reflex|dc:15] each round avoids their contact poison.

D. The Dead Warrens — a derro-infested undercity laboratory beneath Potter's Ward, the last chapter site (the PCs recover the murdered Shoanti boy Gaekhen's body here for Event 13). The entrance matches Elkaris's testimony (@UUID[.lAEtkmbh1g2svBUJ]{Event 13}): a partially collapsed mausoleum near the ward's southern edge, a toppled, headless sword-wielding gargoyle statue in the dirt before it. Wheelbarrow tracks at the surface (@Check[type:perception|dc:15], @Check[type:nature|dc:21] to read the small bare footprints around them as derro) lead behind the gargoyle to a poorly hidden trap-door inside (@Check[type:perception|dc:15]).

GM: This is one of several labs kept by the necromancer Rolth (Gaedren Lamm's estranged son, the 'Key-Lock Killer'). Rolth is absent — away securing a scroll — and is not scheduled to appear; he returns after the PCs leave to find his lair wrecked, and hunts them in Chapter 2. In his stead the warrens are run by his derro apprentice @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vreegDerroNec001]{Vreeg}.

Gaekhen's body is in four parts (Rolth harvested only the head): the legs in the corpse dump (@UUID[.lN66rXLQlv1qqJtX]{D4}), the left arm clutched by the carrion golem (@UUID[.TnexBuoE8QRzKO7Q]{D8}), the head stitched to the flesh golem in the stitchery (@UUID[.7I11UQ9DftM2qYWA]{D11}), and the torso + right arm — crudely animated by Vreeg — twitching on the bench in his chamber (@UUID[.q9TYHeGnxKqUNIRn]{D13}). Thousand Bones needs all four parts, not a whole body.

Chapter Conclusion. With the Dead Warrens cleared and the scapegoat's fate resolved, 'Edge of Anarchy' ends on the attempted execution of an innocent — the PCs' first chance to stand directly against Ileosa's growing power. Run it as an interactive cut-scene, not a standard encounter. The PCs should be well into 4th level.

Returning Gaekhen. The PCs need only deliver Gaekhen's four body parts to Cressida — she handles the delicate work of seeing the body de-animated, made whole, and returned to @UUID[.lAEtkmbh1g2svBUJ]{Thousand Bones} with dignity. She pays the recovery reward (folding in Trinia's deferred coin if applicable), and the Shoanti elder's goodwill becomes important in Chapter 4 (A History of Ashes). She also passes on the news of the sunset execution (@UUID[.FZ53VK84dpCVeiiv]{Event 14}) — and asks the PCs to attend.

The Courtyard at Sunset

The execution it is not an affair to be missed. The toast of Korvosa is in attendance in garish gowns, fine capes, and enough jewels to blind the common citizen. The overall feel of the event is that of a grand ball or party, not an assassin's public execution.

Staging. Heralds announce Queen Ileosa with drums and fanfare; she has fully taken up the mantle of sole monarch — poised, graceful, in a green-and-white silk dress worth thousands — attended by a small army of striking guards. Chief among them, all but unrecognizable in a brand-new suit of full plate with a closed helm and flowing crimson plume, is Sabina (the Gray Maidens' look, before the name exists). Ileosa takes a high throne-like chair at one end of the public courtyard; at the other stands a guillotine, beside it a towering hooded executioner idly holding an immense axe. Gossip ripples through the crowd — the queen's attire, how the traitor will comport herself, the identity of the armored guardian… even the PCs' own exploits, if you like.

The procession. An ominous drumbeat silences the gawkers and paces Trinia's walk to the block. At the guillotine her shackles and hood are removed — a very frightened young woman bravely holding back tears, arms bound behind her with a leather cord — as the queen rises to address the crowd.

If the PCs hid Trinia, the prisoner revealed is a secondary scapegoat — superficially similar, equally innocent. Everything below plays out the same: Blackjack has no intention of letting anyone be falsely executed by the queen.

Queen Ileosa's Address

'Fellow Korvosans! You have suffered greatly these past few weeks. Homes have burned, family members have died, and fortunes have been lost. I feel your suffering, for not only have I lost a beloved husband, but with each act of anarchy that followed, my heart bled more. This has been a trying time for us, yet the torment is at an end. Before you is the source of your anguish and pain. Do not be deceived by this murderer's timid nature-she is a black-hearted assassin. I offer you her death as a salve against the hatred and hurt you have suffered.

'And so, without further delay, let us usher in this new dawn with justice! OFF WITH HER HEAD!'

The rescue. Just before the executioner forces Trinia into the guillotine, he howls — a thrown dagger stands in the back of his hand — and a cry goes up: 'By the gods! It's Blackjack!' A man in hooded cloak and black mask springs to Trinia's side, cuts her bonds, and hurls the same dagger down through the executioner's boot, pinning his foot to the platform. Then he turns to the stunned crowd:

Blackjack's Declaration

'Yes indeed, my queen! Let us usher in justice, but let that be justice for Korvosa, not this shambles you call a monarchy! Long live Korvosa! Down with the queen!'

Chaos. The crowd erupts — some scream for the assassin's head, others for the queen's resignation. Ileosa stands frozen a moment, whispers something to Sabina, and flees into Castle Korvosa with Sabina and a dozen guards covering her retreat. The remaining guards push toward Blackjack through blood-thirsty nobles — while the executioner wrenches free and raises his axe over the vigilante, who, delighting in having routed the queen, hasn't noticed.

Involving the PCs

Don't run this as a battle — go around the table and ask each player what their character does.

  • If the PCs stay out of it (or their actions change little): Blackjack notices the axe in time, ducks away dragging Trinia with him, clambers up a hanging banner to a nearby wall, hauls her up, drinks a potion of feather fall, bows to the crowd backlit by the setting sun — and the two leap from the wall and vanish into the city.
  • If the PCs help Blackjack: narrate how their actions save his life (the axe-blow turned, a guard tripped, the crowd parted). When he reaches the wall, his bow is to the PCs in particular, rapier raised in salute, before he and Trinia leap to freedom.
  • If the PCs hinder him: let them roll attacks as needed. On low rolls, Blackjack and Trinia escape despite their efforts. On high rolls, let the dice stand: Blackjack is captured and unmasked as Vencarlo Orisini before being thrown into Castle Korvosa's deepest dungeons, and Trinia is executed after all — this time in a private chamber in the dungeons below. The remaining chapters assume both escaped; if not, things get harder for the PCs in the chapters to come, as you see fit (Chapter 3's Vencarlo thread in particular needs re-rigging).

Aftermath. Escaping the chaos is easy, and even PCs who visibly helped Blackjack face no Guard retribution — partly the confusion, mostly a shift in the city's heart: Blackjack has always been Korvosa's folk hero, and his open stand against the queen plants the first real doubts among her supporters. Ileosa stays ensconced in Castle Korvosa for several weeks, and the city finally gets room to breathe and rebuild.

Closing — Read Aloud

Yet however safe the city might seem, its troubles are far from over. Korvosa is in the eye of the storm. Greater wheels are turning, and somewhere not too far from shore, a black three-masted vessel flying the orange lanterns of a plague-ship drifts ever closer to shore. Death follows it.

Into Chapter 2 — and the Blood Veil. That plague ship is the Direption, Chapter 2's opening. This is where the persistent-plague overlay begins: start the Epidemic Clock (Conversion Guide journal → Blood Veil & the Epidemic Clock) seeded by how the city fared here. Rolth, returning to his ruined lair, becomes a recurring hunter (Chapter 2).

Threads carried forward: Rolth's vendetta · the Arkona/Vimanda watch (from @UUID[.klh1VxaRSp2p6D65]{Verik}) · Trinia & Vencarlo/Blackjack · Grau and the Korvosan Guard · the Shoanti debt · and the queen, who now has the PCs' measure.
